title: dPCR SOP for Quantification of Antibiotic Resistance Genes
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"cqq79","text":"To describe the procedures on how to set-up digital polymerase chain reaction (dPCR) for selected antibiotic resistance genes and determinants: 16S ribosomal RNA, sulfonamide (sul1), erythromycin (erm(B)), extended spectrum β-lactamase (blaCTX-M-1), integrase (intI1); and tetracycline tet(A), tet(O), and tet(X).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":79,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":176,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":197,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":237,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":261,"length":5},{"style":"italic","offset":286,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":294,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":306,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"7g07o","text":"Scope","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1jkfb","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bii2u","text":"Digital PCR is a specialized approach to nucleic acid detection and quantification that estimates absolute numbers of molecules through statistical methods. Digital PCR (dPCR) is the third-generation PCR technique. It sequesters probes and nucleic acids, and uses the same fundamental chemistry as qPCR, but unlike qPCR data, dPCR data are collected at the endpoint of the reaction mix. Before amplification, a bulk PCR reaction made up of nucleic acid, primers, probes, and master mix is digitized into many thousands of nanoliter-sized microreactions. As this digitization process distributes the PCR mix across so many microreactions, each microreaction will effectively either contain one, zero, or just a handful of the target nucleic acid molecules (ThermoFisher, 2015).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d2g56","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3jluc","text":"The isolated microreactions are then amplified, and data are collected from each microreaction at the end of the thermal cycling process. Microreactions that do not contain the target will not show post-amplification fluorescence, while those that do contain the target will show post-amplification fluorescence (ThermoFisher, 2015). While the concept of reading the fluorescent signals from amplified genes is the same compared to qPCR, dPCR is better at preserving the initial reaction mix conditions and clearly representing amplification progress, minimizing the occurrence of primer-dimers, and false amplification reading (Park, 2021). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6o7j5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cnpb1","text":"The QIAcuity Digital PCR System is designed to deliver precise and multiplexed quantification results for mutation detection, copy number variation (CNV), gene expression studies, gene-editing analysis, and many more. This nanoplate-based system seamlessly integrates a standard dPCR workflow of partitioning, thermocycling and imaging into a walk-away automated platform with minimal hands-on time (Qiagen, 2024).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bhqos","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5vod4","text":"Responsibility ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":15}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6ufb9","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bd8bl","text":"This SOP applies to all staff members and students. These individuals must be knowledgeable about the requirements set forth within this document. The lab manager or designee shall ensure that all staff and students know the proper techniques. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7p973","text":" Appendix B – gBlock steps: Ensure that you wear gloves when working with a plate and do not apply force to it.
The QIAcuity reads fluorescence from the bottom of the plate, which is covered with a foil. For best results, keep the foil clean and avoid damage such as scratches.
For better plate handling, place the nanoplate into the nanoplate tray.
Keep the barcode on the side of the plate clean and intact.
For transport of the finished Nanoplate to the QIAcuity instrument, the plate should be held at the side edges or on the tray horizontally.
Transport smoothly without shaking or turning the plate, to ensure that the reaction mix is at the bottom of the input well.
DO NOT store the plate for more than 2 hours before the start of a run as this may lead to pre-priming of the reaction mix resulting in a reduced number of analyzable partitions.
The plate can be stored at Room temperature or at 4 °C after the run for up to 1 week. You can re-image a plate up to 7 times.
Prepare the dPCR prep area for setting up a dPCR reaction to eliminate contamination – UV the plate, PCR strip-tubes, PCR water, and microcentrifuge tubes for 00:15:00 .

Thaw the QIAcuity PCR master mix, template DNA, primers (& probes for probe-based reactions), Q-Solution, and RNase-free water. Vigorously mix the QIAcuity PCR master mix and the individual solutions. Centrifuge briefly to collect liquids at the bottom of the tubes.
QIAcuity Probe mastermix for probe-based reactions.
QIAcuity EG mastermix for non-probe-based reactions.
Prepare a reaction mix for the number of reactions needed according to Tables 1a Probe & 1b EG . %s %s %s %s
Due to the hot-start polymerase, it is not necessary to keep reagents On ice during reaction setup or while programming the QIAcuity instrument.
Mix the primers and probe by aspirating and expelling with the pipette tip, then add to the reaction mix.
Vortex the reaction mix.

Dispense appropriate volumes according to Tables 1a Probe & 1b EG of the reaction mix, into the wells of a standard PCR plate or 0.2µL PCR tubes. Then, add template DNA (See Appendix A go to step #24 for making dilutions) into each well that contains the reaction mix according to Tables 1a Probe & 1b EG .
To mix, pipette up and down at least 4 times. Seal the standard PCR pre-plate or tubes, vortex, and centrifuge briefly.
DO NOT pipette the master mix and the sample separately into the nanoplate, as this will lead to insufficient mixing.
Transfer the content of each well from the standard PCR plate/tubes to the wells of the nanoplate.
To ensure bubble-free pipetting:
DO NOT blow-out the liquid at the end of each dispense into the nanoplate.
Aspirate 40 µL from each pre-plate well for dispensing into the bottom of each well of the 24-well nanoplate.
Aspirate 15 µL from each pre-plate well for dispensing into the bottom of each well of the 96-well nanoplate.

Seal the nanoplate properly using the nanoplate seal. For a properly sealed plate, the plate seal covers the whole structure, and no bubbles or strong depressions are visible , as this can also lead to bad filling of the wells.
Remove the bottom white protective foil from the seal.
Center and align the plate seal so the foil doesn’t overlap on any side more than 1 mm.
Fix the seal with the nanoplate roller in the horizontal and vertical direction.
Remove the upper protective foil, beginning at the bottom left corner.
Hold the plate seal in place while lifting the upper transparent foil off.
Fix the seal again, this time with high force, rolling at least 3 times forwards and backwards horizontally then vertically. Roll over the plate seal covering the nanoplate frame.
Ensure that overlapping parts of the plate seal are turned down and well attached to the plate frame and that the barcode is not covered. DO NOT apply pressure to the plate seal.
The plate can only be labeled in the lane between the plate edge and the printed letters, next to column 1, as well as the mirrored portion next to column 12.
DO NOT mark the bottom side of the plate , as it is used to read fluorescent signals.
If a restriction enzyme for DNA digestion has been included in the reaction, leave the plate for 00:10:00 at Room temperature .
Proceed to [Section 'Procedure – Thermal Cycling Conditions & Software Set-up' go to step #17 ] to program the cycler of the QIAcuity instrument according to Tables 2a Probe & 2b EG . %s %s %s %s
Open and login to the QIAcuity Software Suite.
Click Plates in the main toolbar to enter the plates environment.
Click New Plate.
Specify General Data → Plate Name, Plate Type, Barcode. (Optional to enter Description and Labels ).
When scanning or entering the barcode this will automatically fill in Plate Type . Each barcode is unique and can only be used once.
Specify dPCR Parameters → Priming, Cycling, Imaging.
Priming : priming parameters are plate type specific. Select the applicable Priming Profile after entering the Plate Type in General Data.

Cycling: Define the temperature profile of your experiment (See Table 2 ). In the Temperature field, specify the temperature of the step, then specify the duration of the temperature in the Duration field. Click Add Temperature Step. Repeat these steps for all temperature steps, using the up and down arrows to arrange the order of the steps. Check the box corresponding to the temperature steps that you want to use for repeated cycling and click Group . In the first column of the grouped temperature steps, add the number of cycles.
Imaging : enables you to set the respective exposure duration and gain value for each channel.
Select the channel(s) relevant for your experiment. For FAM probe-based & EG genes select Green .

Set the ‘Exposure Duration’ and ‘Gain,’ then click Save Changes or Done . For Probe -based: Typically, Exposure should be at 500 ms and Gain at 6 . You can add additional ‘Imaging’ segments and change the parameters (a Gain of 10 is another gain to try out). If you need to troubleshoot, you can play around with the exposure and gain. For EG : Typically, Exposure should be at 300 ms and Gain at 3 . You can add additional ‘Imaging’ segments and change the parameters. If you need to troubleshoot, you can play around with the exposure and gain.
Specify the Reaction Mixes.

Select New Reaction Mix . Type in ‘Reaction mix name’ and ‘Target Name’ which can just be the gene you are using. 1. Select the ‘Dye.’ For Table 2a genes select FAM . For Table 2b genes select EvaGreen . 2. Select the ‘Channel’. For Table 2a,b genes select Green .
Specify the Plate Layout.
Highlight all the wells and select the Reaction Mix created in Step 21.

Individually select each well and create a ‘New Sample’ – input the sample ID and other identifiable information (dilution, rep, etc.). Input Template Volume (typically 5µL or 10µL ), Total Reaction Volume ( 15µL or 40µL ), and Concentration Factor (aka the sample dilution). gBlock standards can be input as a sample. Non-Template Control (NTC) input as a Non-Template Control – create new, and type NTC .
Load the plate into the machine and start a run.
Turn on and log in to the machine. The plate’s layout and parameters must be completed in the Software Suite before starting a run.
To eject a tray, press the physical button on the instrument or tap Eject Tray on the touch screen.

Place a plate in the ejected slot with the barcode facing toward the rear of the instrument. Ensure that the top seal of the plate is intact and not overlapping on the side more than 1 mm in any direction. Close the tray-slot.

The instrument will scan the barcode, and once detected in the closed slot, the touchscreen will indicate that Plate is detected . If the barcode matches to an existing experiment in the Software Suite, the loaded plate plan displays the defined run setup and can be started.
You can double check plate information and view details for the plate by clicking Show details in the plate’s pane.
Click the Run icon on the plate’s pane to start the run.

The Running Status of the run can be tracked throughout the whole run cycle. Status bar with time running is displayed. Font color of completed steps are black. When a step is in progress, its font color is blue. Pending steps are shown in light gray. A run can be stopped at any time by pressing the Stop Run icon. Aborting a plate during the priming/rolling steps renders it unusable and the plate cannot be used and run again. A plate that has been aborted during the Thermocycling or Imaging step can be used again. To rerun the plate, configure a run with only the remaining steps.
When the run is finished, the status changes to Done and the Eject button becomes available.
Prior to running a full plate of samples, test a subset at varying dilutions to see which will give the best result ( Appendix C – The dMIQE Group in 'Guidelines' ).
From the straight DNA create a 10-fold, 100-fold, and 1000-fold dilution using the increments of 1 µL DNA and 9 µL PCR water at the volume desired.
Run a plate of the test dilutions. Can also include the straight DNA if desired.

After seeing the results, determine the best dilution for the sample set, or alter based on nanodrop information. Then make that dilution for your sample set and continue with section - 'Procedure – Reaction Set-up' (Step 7) . Redo and go out further dilutions if necessary.
